name: Tacttoo
short_description: A sub-35-micrometer feel-through temporary tattoo that delivers high-density electro-tactile output on the skin.
long_description: Tacttoo is a research prototype from Saarland University's HCI Lab that integrates electro-tactile electrodes into an ultra-thin temporary tattoo (under 35 micrometers thick) that conforms to complex body geometry such as a fingertip. It is 'feel-through': it preserves the wearer's natural tactile acuity while superimposing computer-generated electro-tactile stimuli, enabling tactile augmented reality, private notifications, and on-skin interaction. As physical AI and wearable interaction design, it matters because it pushes haptic output to a near-imperceptible epidermal layer, reframing the body's surface itself as a high-resolution display channel.
ai_note: Tacttoo is an electro-tactile output and fabrication technique; the paper describes no AI/ML running in the system.

images.caption: A Tacttoo electro-tactile temporary tattoo applied to a fingertip.
images.alt_text: A close-up of a fingertip with a thin, nearly transparent printed-circuit tattoo bearing small electrodes conforming to the skin.

sources.title: Tacttoo: A Thin and Feel-Through Tattoo for On-Skin Tactile Output
sources.publisher: ACM (Proceedings of the 31st Annual ACM Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology, UIST '18)

sources.title: Tacttoo - Human-Computer Interaction Lab, Saarland University
Tacttoo: A Thin and Feel-Through Tattoo for On-Skin Tactile Output (author PDF)

interactions.title: Feel a car's engine buzz through a clay model
interactions.description: Wearing Tacttoo on the index fingertip, a designer runs a finger over a physical 3D car model and feels the real rubbery tire and surface ridges, while electro-tactile taxels simultaneously overlay the simulated engine vibration at the exact frequency that part of the car would vibrate.
interactions.source_label: Tacttoo paper (UIST '18), Applications: Tactile AR on 3D Models

interactions.title: Press a sketched button and feel it click back
interactions.description: A paper UI sketch is captured by camera; when the Tacttoo-wearing finger touches a drawn button it gets a short burst of taxel vibration like a 'click', and dragging along a sketched slider renders continuous feedback tracking the knob's position.
interactions.source_label: Tacttoo paper (UIST '18), Applications: Augmenting Paper Prototypes

interactions.title: Find eyes-free music controls by a vein on your forearm
interactions.description: Play/pause buttons are rendered on the forearm centered on a natural landmark (a vein the user can still feel through the tattoo); touching each spot fires a distinct vibration frequency so the user identifies and triggers controls without looking.
interactions.source_label: Tacttoo paper (UIST '18), Applications: Dynamic Tactile Landmarks

interactions.title: Touch a virtual tree and feel its branches
interactions.description: In a Unity VR scene with the fingertip tracked, reaching out to a virtual object renders its geometry on the skin: sharp branch tips fire as single low-frequency taxels while flat leaf surfaces actuate multiple taxels at high frequency.
interactions.source_label: Tacttoo paper (UIST '18), Applications: Tactile Rendering in VR

interactions.title: Get a silent, invisible alert in a meeting
interactions.description: Worn under clothing or on the inner forearm, Tacttoo signals an incoming message, call, or upcoming event with distinct electro-tactile patterns and no audible noise, so the wearer feels the notification privately mid-conversation while no one else notices.

interactions.title: Tap your own skin as both the button and the buzz
interactions.description: A single forearm Tacttoo combines a capacitive touch sensor with active taxel areas, so touching a rendered landmark both registers the input and answers with a tactile cue from the same self-contained patch — no separate tracker or device needed.
interactions.source_label: Tacttoo paper (UIST '18), Applications: integrated sensing + output

interactions.title: Guided navigation cues at a crosswalk
interactions.description: Because Tacttoo can place distinct, localized vibration patterns on the fingertip without blocking real-world touch, a wayfinding app could nudge 'turn left' or 'stop' onto the skin while the wearer still feels the cane, railing, or curb edge they are using.

interactions.title: Feel the texture of an online product before buying
interactions.description: Grounded in Tacttoo's per-taxel rendering of surface roughness and edges, a shopping app could let you run a fingertip over a screen image of a fabric or grip and feel a coarse approximation of its texture rendered on your skin.
